description: Web Development interview questions for junior or entry-level candidates.

questions.question: Why is semantic HTML important in web development?

choices.text: It makes every website load without JavaScript.
It helps browsers, assistive technologies, and search engines understand the structure and meaning of the page.
It automatically makes a website responsive on all devices.
It removes the need to write CSS.

questions.correctExplanation: Semantic HTML uses meaningful elements like header, nav, main, section, and button. This improves accessibility, readability, and SEO because the page structure is easier to understand.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Semantic HTML does not remove the need for JavaScript. It mainly improves structure and meaning.
Responsive design usually requires CSS techniques such as media queries, flexbox, or grid.
Semantic HTML and CSS solve different problems. CSS is still needed for styling.

questions.question: When is Flexbox usually a good choice in CSS layout?

choices.text: When arranging items in one direction, such as a row or a column.
Only when building database tables.
When replacing all JavaScript logic.
Only when creating animations.

questions.correctExplanation: Flexbox is especially useful for one-dimensional layouts, such as aligning items in a row or column and controlling spacing and alignment.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Flexbox is a CSS layout tool, not something specifically for database tables.
Flexbox handles layout, not application logic.
Flexbox can be used with animated elements, but animation is not its main purpose.

questions.question: What is a common reason to choose CSS Grid instead of Flexbox?

choices.text: CSS Grid is better for two-dimensional layouts with both rows and columns.
CSS Grid automatically connects the frontend to the backend.
CSS Grid only works for mobile screens.
CSS Grid replaces HTML elements.

questions.correctExplanation: CSS Grid is strong for two-dimensional layouts where you need to control both rows and columns, such as dashboards, galleries, or complex page sections.

wrongExplanations.explanation: CSS Grid is only a layout system. It does not handle backend connections.
CSS Grid works across many screen sizes, not only mobile.
CSS Grid styles HTML elements; it does not replace them.

questions.question: What is the DOM in web development?

choices.text: A database used by JavaScript.
A programming language for backend servers.
A browser representation of the HTML document that JavaScript can read and modify.
A CSS framework for responsive layouts.

questions.correctExplanation: The DOM, or Document Object Model, represents the page structure in the browser. JavaScript can use it to read, update, add, or remove elements.

wrongExplanations.explanation: The DOM is not a database. It is the browser's representation of the document.
The DOM is not a programming language.
The DOM is not a CSS framework.

questions.question: Why are async and await commonly used in JavaScript?

choices.text: To write asynchronous code in a more readable way, especially when working with promises.
To make CSS load faster.
To prevent all runtime errors automatically.
To convert JavaScript into HTML.

questions.correctExplanation: async and await help developers write asynchronous code that looks more like synchronous code, making API calls and promise-based logic easier to read and maintain.

wrongExplanations.explanation: async and await are JavaScript features and do not directly optimize CSS loading.
They do not prevent all errors. You still need error handling such as try/catch.
They do not convert JavaScript into HTML.

questions.question: A frontend page needs to load data from a server. What should you usually handle when using fetch?

choices.text: Only the successful response, because failed requests are impossible.
Loading state, successful response, error handling, and possibly empty data.
Only CSS styling for the response.
Only database schema changes.

questions.correctExplanation: A good frontend implementation should handle the full request lifecycle: loading, success, error, and empty states. This improves reliability and user experience.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Network requests can fail, so ignoring errors creates poor UX.
Styling is only one part of the UI. Data fetching also requires state and error handling.
Database schema changes are backend concerns and are not the main task when using fetch on the frontend.

questions.question: What is a common reason to use state in React?

choices.text: To store values that can change and cause the UI to update.
To permanently store data in a database.
To write CSS without class names.
To make every component global automatically.

questions.correctExplanation: React state is used for values that change over time and should affect what the user sees, such as form input, filters, loading state, or selected items.

wrongExplanations.explanation: React state is stored in the UI layer, not permanently in a database.
State is not a CSS replacement.
React state is local by default unless you explicitly lift it or use a global state solution.

questions.question: What are props in React?

choices.text: Values passed from a parent component to a child component.
A way to directly modify the database.
A replacement for all HTML elements.
A browser API for storing cookies.

questions.correctExplanation: Props allow parent components to pass data or callbacks to child components, making components reusable and configurable.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Props are part of React component communication, not database modification.
Props configure components; they do not replace HTML.
Props are not a browser storage API.

questions.question: What is a common use case for useEffect in React?

choices.text: To run side effects such as fetching data, subscribing to events, or syncing with external systems.
To define the HTML structure of the entire application.
To create database tables.
To replace all component props.

questions.correctExplanation: useEffect is used for side effects that happen outside the normal rendering process, such as data fetching, subscriptions, timers, or interacting with browser APIs.

wrongExplanations.explanation: HTML structure is usually returned from the component's render output, not from useEffect.
Creating database tables is not a React useEffect responsibility.
useEffect does not replace props. Props and effects serve different purposes.

questions.question: In a Next.js App Router project, what is the purpose of the app directory?

choices.text: It defines routes, layouts, pages, and route handlers using a file-system based structure.
It stores only image files.
It replaces the need for React components.
It is used only for writing database migrations.

questions.correctExplanation: The app directory in Next.js App Router organizes routes, pages, layouts, loading states, error boundaries, and route handlers based on the file system.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Images may be stored elsewhere, such as public, but the app directory is for routing and app structure.
Next.js still uses React components.
Database migrations are not the purpose of the app directory.

questions.question: In Next.js App Router, when should you usually use a Client Component?

choices.text: When the component needs browser-only APIs, local state, event handlers, or interactive behavior.
For every component, even when no interactivity is needed.
Only for database queries.
Only for static text that never changes.

questions.correctExplanation: Client Components are needed when a component uses hooks like useState, handles user events, accesses browser APIs, or requires interactivity.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Using Client Components everywhere can increase client-side JavaScript unnecessarily.
Database queries should usually happen on the server, not inside Client Components.
Static text usually does not require a Client Component.

questions.question: What is a main benefit of using TypeScript in a web project?

choices.text: It can catch many type-related errors before runtime.
It automatically designs the user interface.
It removes the need for testing.
It makes all code run only on the server.

questions.correctExplanation: TypeScript adds static typing, which helps catch type errors earlier and improves code maintainability, especially in larger projects.

wrongExplanations.explanation: TypeScript does not design UI automatically.
TypeScript helps reduce some bugs, but testing is still necessary.
TypeScript does not decide whether code runs on the client or server.

questions.question: A user submits a form with missing required fields. What should a good web application do?

choices.text: Silently ignore the submission.
Show clear validation messages and help the user correct the input.
Crash the page so the user knows something is wrong.
Save the incomplete data without warning.

questions.correctExplanation: Good form UX clearly explains what needs to be fixed and helps the user complete the form successfully.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Ignoring the submission creates confusion and poor user experience.
Crashing the page is not a professional way to handle validation.
Saving incomplete data can create data quality and workflow problems.

questions.question: Which practice improves accessibility for users who navigate with a keyboard?

choices.text: Removing all focus outlines because they look distracting.
Making interactive elements focusable and ensuring visible focus states.
Using only images instead of text.
Making buttons work only on mouse hover.

questions.correctExplanation: Keyboard users need to move through interactive elements and see where focus is. Visible focus states and proper focus behavior are essential for accessibility.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Removing focus outlines can make navigation difficult or impossible for keyboard users.
Images without text alternatives can reduce accessibility.
Hover-only interactions do not work well for keyboard or touch users.

questions.question: A web page feels slow because it loads too much JavaScript on the client. What is one reasonable improvement?

choices.text: Move appropriate work to the server and avoid making every component a Client Component.
Add more client-side libraries to handle the slowness.
Remove all CSS from the project.
Ignore performance unless users complain directly.

questions.correctExplanation: Reducing unnecessary client-side JavaScript can improve performance. In frameworks like Next.js, using Server Components where appropriate can help keep the client bundle smaller.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Adding more client-side libraries may increase JavaScript size and make the problem worse.
Removing CSS is not a meaningful solution to excessive JavaScript.
Performance should be considered proactively because it affects user experience.

questions.question: Why should user input be validated on the server even if it is already validated on the client?

choices.text: Because client-side validation can be bypassed.
Because server-side validation makes CSS easier.
Because browsers cannot display form errors.
Because validation is only useful for animations.

questions.correctExplanation: Client-side validation improves UX, but it can be bypassed. Server-side validation is necessary to protect data integrity and security.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Server-side validation is not related to making CSS easier.
Browsers can display form errors, but that does not replace server-side validation.
Validation is about correctness, safety, and data quality, not animations.

questions.question: What is authentication in a web application?

choices.text: Checking who the user is, such as by login credentials or OAuth.
Deciding the color theme of the app.
Compressing images for performance.
Sorting database records alphabetically.

questions.correctExplanation: Authentication verifies a user's identity. It answers the question: who is this user?

wrongExplanations.explanation: Color theme is a UI concern, not authentication.
Image compression is a performance optimization, not authentication.
Sorting records is a data operation, not authentication.

questions.question: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?

choices.text: Authentication checks who the user is, while authorization checks what the user is allowed to do.
They are exactly the same thing.
Authorization only means logging out.
Authentication only applies to CSS files.

questions.correctExplanation: Authentication verifies identity. Authorization determines permissions, such as whether a user can access or modify a resource.

wrongExplanations.explanation: They are related but not the same.
Authorization is about permissions, not logging out.
Authentication is a security concept and is not limited to CSS.

questions.question: In a REST API, what is a common purpose of the GET method?

choices.text: To retrieve data from the server.
To permanently delete all records.
To style a page.
To compile TypeScript.

questions.correctExplanation: GET is commonly used to request or retrieve data from a server without changing it.

wrongExplanations.explanation: DELETE is commonly used for deleting resources, not GET.
Styling is handled by CSS, not REST methods.
Compiling TypeScript is unrelated to REST API methods.

questions.question: Why is it important to design database relationships carefully in a full-stack application?

choices.text: Because relationships affect how data is stored, queried, and kept consistent.
Because relationships only change the font size.
Because databases do not support related data.
Because frontend components cannot render without database relationships.

questions.correctExplanation: Good relationship design helps keep data consistent and makes queries easier to reason about. Poor design can lead to duplication, missing data, or complex queries.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Database relationships do not control font size.
Many databases support related data through tables, keys, or document references.
Frontend components can render many kinds of data, but database relationships affect data modeling, not rendering itself.

questions.question: A feature is not working as expected. What is a good debugging approach?

choices.text: Randomly change code until the problem disappears.
Reproduce the issue, isolate the likely cause, inspect logs or state, and test a targeted fix.
Delete the feature immediately.
Assume the framework is broken.

questions.correctExplanation: A structured debugging approach saves time and helps avoid introducing new bugs. Reproduction, isolation, inspection, and verification are key steps.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Random changes can create new bugs and make the issue harder to understand.
Deleting the feature is usually not an appropriate first step.
Framework bugs are possible but less common than application-level issues.

questions.question: Why is it useful to write tests for important application logic?

choices.text: Tests help confirm expected behavior and reduce the risk of breaking features during future changes.
Tests guarantee that the application has no bugs.
Tests replace the need to understand the code.
Tests are only useful for CSS colors.

questions.correctExplanation: Tests are valuable because they document expected behavior and help catch regressions when code changes.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Tests reduce risk but cannot guarantee a completely bug-free application.
Tests support understanding, but developers still need to understand the code.
Tests can cover many types of logic, not only visual styles.
